title: The Trigger in Receiving Feedback
researcherTopic: sensorimotor psychotherapy, the body in trauma treatment
metaDescription: Learn why feedback triggers a trauma response and how to navigate it. Explore the somatic reasons behind the reaction and find a path to healing.
imageDescription: A pair of hands gently hold a broken ceramic bowl, mended with shimmering gold, embodying the concept of kintsugi. The scene is bathed in warm, soft morning light, creating a luminous and healing atmosphere.
htmlContent:
Nobody warns you that the most well-intentioned feedback will feel like a threat assessment, not because the words are wrong, but because the body is listening to a conversation from a past it has not yet been able to leave behind. It is a conversation encoded not in language but in sensation, a somatic echo chamber where every piece of constructive criticism, every gentle suggestion, every performance review reverberates with the frequency of a much older, more foundational danger. This isn’t a failure of intellect or a lack of resilience; it is the predictable, physiological inheritance of a nervous system organized around survival in the face of relational threat. The trigger is not the feedback itself, but the sudden, visceral collapse of time.

We believe we are responding to the present moment, to a manager or a partner or a friend, but the body is already reacting to a ghost—a parent’s conditional approval, a caregiver’s unpredictable moods, the felt sense of needing to be perfect to be safe. The architecture of complex trauma is such that the past is never truly past; it is a living, breathing presence in the tissues of the body. It shapes our perception from the inside out, turning a simple observation into a verdict on our very worthiness to exist. The work, then, is not to learn to ‘take feedback better’ in the conventional sense, but to understand the body’s profound and intelligent reasons for rejecting it.

The Body’s Archive of Unsolicited Reports

Long before the thinking mind attempts to parse the words being offered, the nervous system has already made its own assessment, a process Stephen Porges termed neuroception. It is a constant, subliminal scanning of the environment for cues of safety or danger, operating far below the threshold of conscious thought. For a system conditioned by developmental trauma—by experiences of neglect, enmeshment, or chronic misattunement—the default bias is toward perceiving threat. Feedback, in this context, is not merely data; it is a potent relational stimulus that can instantly signal a shift in connection, a potential withdrawal of approval, or the looming specter of abandonment. The body doesn’t hear ‘you could improve this section’; it senses ‘you are not enough, and therefore you are not safe.’

You cannot think your way into a felt sense of safety. The body has its own logic.

This is the crux of the issue, the place where popular advice about communication and professional development falls so painfully short. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen countless individuals who can intellectually grasp that the feedback is not a personal attack, yet their bodies react as if it is. The somatic response—the tightening in the chest, the heat rising in the face, the sudden urge to disappear or lash out—is not a cognitive choice. It is the activation of a deeply ingrained procedural memory, a survival strategy that was once necessary. The body remembers the high stakes of another’s evaluation. It remembers when feedback was directly tethered to love, to safety, to belonging. To ask it to simply ignore that history is to misunderstand its fundamental language.

When Every Critique Confirms a Core Wound

The triggered reaction to feedback is rarely just about the feedback itself; it is about the story it appears to confirm, the core wound it scrapes against. For many who have navigated the landscape of C-PTSD, there exists a foundational belief of being somehow flawed, deficient, or unworthy. This is not narcissism or ego; it is the internalized residue of an environment that failed to provide consistent, unconditional mirroring. The inner critic, a powerful and often relentless internal voice, seizes upon external feedback as evidence for its case. It’s a devastatingly efficient process—the external data point is immediately assimilated into a pre-existing negative feedback loop, reinforcing the very belief that causes the most pain.

The conversation becomes internal almost instantly. The other person’s words are merely the catalyst for a much harsher, more familiar diatribe from the critic within. We are no longer in a dialogue with someone else; we are trapped in a monologue with our own deepest fears. This is why the content of the feedback often becomes irrelevant. It could be delivered with immense skill and kindness, yet the nervous system has already flagged it as a threat, and the inner critic has already begun its cross-examination. We are defending a case that was closed long ago, arguing against a verdict that was delivered in childhood. The work here is not about winning the argument, but about recognizing who is speaking. It involves a gentle but firm turning of attention from the content of the critique to the process of the reaction itself. We must learn to see the critic not as a truth-teller, but as a terrified part of us trying to protect us from future hurt by keeping us small. Understanding this internal system is the first step toward disarming the trigger.

There's a difference between being alone and being with yourself. One is circumstance. The other is practice.

The Somatic Echo of a Red Pen

The experience of being triggered by feedback is profoundly physical. It’s the sudden, unwelcome heat that floods the cheeks, the metallic taste in the mouth, the way the stomach clenches as if bracing for a blow. These are not metaphors; they are the physiological markers of a nervous system shifting into a state of defense. This is the domain of what researcher Pat Ogden, a pioneer in the field of sensorimotor psychotherapy, calls procedural memory. It is the body’s memory of what to do in the face of threat: the impulse to fight, to flee, to freeze, or to fawn. These are not conscious decisions but automated, deeply learned responses that bypass the neocortex, the thinking part of our brain, entirely.

When feedback is received, the body doesn’t just hear the words; it feels the echo of every past experience where evaluation was dangerous. It feels the teacher’s red pen, the parent’s disappointed sigh, the sting of being compared to a sibling. These memories are stored not as a coherent narrative but as a constellation of sensations, postures, and impulses. A client once described this to me as feeling like a ghost was pushing on her chest, a heavy, silencing pressure that made it impossible to speak. Through the lens of sensorimotor psychotherapy, the work is not to talk about the feeling, but to notice it, to track it, to get curious about it. What is the sensation? Where does it live in the body? What does it want to do? By bringing mindful attention to the somatic level, we can begin to separate the past from the present and offer the body a new experience—one where it can feel the trigger without being completely hijacked by it. It is a slow, patient process of renegotiating the terms of our own embodiment.

Silence is not the absence of noise. It's the presence of attention.

De-Coupling the Messenger from the Message

The path toward transforming our relationship with feedback lies in our ability to create a sliver of space between the stimulus and our habitual reaction. It is in this sacred pause that we can begin the delicate work of de-coupling the information being offered from the overwhelming somatic and emotional charge it carries. This does not happen through force of will or by telling ourselves to ‘calm down.’ It happens by cultivating a different kind of attention, one that is rooted in the body and grounded in the present moment. The first step is simply to notice the activation as it arises, without judgment and without needing it to be different. To feel the heat in the face, the clenching in the gut, and to name it internally: ‘Ah, this is the trigger. This is the body’s memory of threat.’

This act of naming and noticing is not a small thing; it is the beginning of dis-identification. We are moving from being the reaction to witnessing the reaction. From this place of witness, we can then begin to sort what belongs to the present from what belongs to the past. We can ask, ‘Is this threat real, right now, in this room? Or is my body responding to an echo?’ In this space, we can also start to separate the messenger from the message. Perhaps the person delivering the feedback is clumsy, or perhaps they are skillful. But their delivery is a separate variable from the information itself. Can we get curious about the information, even if the 90% of the feedback that might be useful, even if the other 90% is a projection? This is not about invalidating our reaction; it is about expanding our capacity to hold both our reaction and a sense of curiosity at the same time. It’s a practice, not a perfectible skill. It’s about building a more resilient internal structure, one breath at a time.

The Architecture of a Secure Internal Witness

Ultimately, the work of healing the feedback trigger is the work of cultivating a secure internal attachment. It is about developing an inner witness that is more constant, more compassionate, and more reliable than any external source of validation could ever be. This is not the same as self-esteem, which is often contingent on performance and external approval. This is a deeper, more abiding sense of self that is not contingent on anything. It is the felt sense of being on our own side, no matter what. This witness doesn’t argue with the inner critic or try to silence the triggered parts; it simply offers a broader perspective. It acknowledges the pain of the reaction while simultaneously holding the truth that our worth is not up for debate.

Building this internal architecture is the central project of healing complex trauma. It involves practices that bring us back into relationship with ourselves—not just our thoughts, but our bodies, our emotions, our deepest truths. It can look like a daily meditation practice, not to quiet the mind, but to become familiar with its patterns. It can look like a commitment to somatic inquiry, learning to listen to the subtle language of the body. It can look like journaling, not to vent, but to untangle the stories we tell ourselves. As we engage in these practices, we are slowly, patiently, rewiring the nervous system for safety and connection. We are teaching it, through experience, that we can handle the discomfort of feedback because we are no longer alone with it. We have ourselves. This is the foundation from which we can begin to engage with the world, and with feedback, not as a defendant on trial, but as a student in a classroom. For a deeper exploration of this, one might turn to the practice of Internal Family Systems.

You don’t arrive at peace. You stop walking away from it.

Feedback as Presence, Not as a Verdict

What if we could re-imagine feedback entirely? Not as a verdict on our past performance, but as an invitation into a deeper presence with ourselves in the present moment. What if the trigger, instead of being a sign that something is wrong with us, is actually a signal pointing us directly toward the unhealed places that need our attention the most? From this perspective, the goal is not to become immune to feedback, to develop a thick skin that nothing can penetrate. The goal is to become so exquisitely sensitive, so profoundly intimate with our own inner landscape, that we can meet the stimulus of feedback with a grounded and open heart. It is a shift from reactivity to receptivity, from defense to discernment.

This receptivity is not passive. It is an active, engaged presence. It requires us to be anchored in our own bodies, to feel our feet on the floor, to track the rhythm of our own breath. From this anchor, we can listen to what is being said without being immediately swept away by the current of our own history. We can learn to hold the discomfort without needing to extinguish it. We can say, ‘Thank you for sharing that. I need some time to sit with it.’ We are creating a buffer, a sacred pause where our own wisdom can emerge. In this space, we can discover what is true for us, what we are willing to integrate, and what we can allow to pass by. We are no longer at the mercy of others’ opinions, because we are rooted in a deeper source of knowing. This is the essence of what it means to cultivate a path of conscious healing, a journey supported by the ongoing work of self-awareness and inner exploration offered at Kalesh.love.

You are not a problem to be solved. You are a process to be witnessed.

The Unfolding Path of Embodied Awareness

The journey from being triggered by feedback to being able to use it as a tool for growth is not a short one, and it is not linear. It is a spiral path that asks us to revisit the same core wounds again and again, each time with a little more awareness, a little more compassion, a little more capacity to stay with ourselves in the fire of discomfort. The trigger itself becomes the guide, the very thing that illuminates where the work is. It is a profound and demanding practice that requires us to be more committed to our own liberation than we are to our own comfort. It asks everything of us. But what might become possible if we were willing to meet the next piece of feedback not as a threat to be defended against, but as a question we were finally ready to live into?

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does even positive feedback sometimes feel unsafe?

For a nervous system wired for threat, any form of attention—even positive—can feel dangerous. If visibility in childhood was often followed by criticism or unpredictable behavior from caregivers, the system learns that being seen is a precursor to being hurt. Positive feedback can therefore trigger a confusing mix of pleasure and a deep, somatic sense of dread, as the body braces for the ‘other shoe to drop.’

How can I handle feedback in a professional setting without revealing my trauma history?

The key is to have a pre-planned, simple script. Something like, ‘Thank you for this perspective. I’m going to take some time to process it and will get back to you if I have questions.’ This response is professional, buys you time, and honors your need to regulate your system before responding. It creates a crucial pause between the stimulus and a potential reaction, allowing you to attend to your body’s needs in private.

Is it possible to ever fully ‘heal’ this trigger?

Healing in the context of complex trauma is less about eradication and more about integration. The goal isn’t to never be triggered again, but for the trigger to have less power, less duration, and less of a grip on your sense of self. Over time, with consistent practice, the reaction becomes a smaller and smaller wave in a much larger ocean of awareness, rather than a tsunami that wipes everything out.

What happens next is up to you.
